what is the name of the phenomenon that would make me choose a over b because i was influenced by a trend?  i've looked at these 1,2,3 and i don't think they answer my question fully.  let's say it's election time and i'm on the fence about who to vote for. i see some survey on the news and seeing party a has more vote intentions, it tends to sway my decision towards party a.  closer to home, i see a question or answer on se and i may feel swayed to vote +1 or -1 if there are pre-existing votes that have been cast.  what would this phenomenon be called (if there is a name for it other than simple influence or conformity)? also, can there be a bias for going against what the current trend is? what is the name of the phenomenon that would make me choose a over b because i was influenced by a trend?  i've looked at these 1,2,3 and i don't think they answer my question fully.  let's say it's election time and i'm on the fence about who to vote for. i see some survey on the news and seeing party a has more vote intentions, it tends to sway my decision towards party a.  closer to home, i see a question or answer on se and i may feel swayed to vote +1 or -1 if there are pre-existing votes that have been cast.  what would this phenomenon be called (if there is a name for it other than simple influence or conformity)? also, can there be a bias for going against what the current trend is? the phenomenon you are describing is commonly known as **"the bandwagon effect."** the bandwagon effect is a psychological phenomenon in which people tend to adopt certain behaviors, follow trends, or vote for candidates primarily because they perceive that many others are doing so. in the context of elections, when a voter sees that party a is leading in polls or has more visible support, they may feel compelled to vote for party a simply because it appears to be the popular or winning side. this can occur even if they were previously undecided or neutral.

similarly, the bandwagon effect can be observed in online communities such as stack exchange (se), these documents are mostly about confusion in life choices, passion, and personal problems. they do not contain relevant psychological terms or concepts addressing conformity, majority influence, bandwagon effect, or related biases. documents 4– these are transcripts and advice mostly concerning

what is the learning/memory type which requires active contribution called?  i observed i best memorize information when i actively compile it into meaningful hierarchy. that encompasses creating powerpoint presentations, explaining the topic to someone or writing summaries. i think my active contribution is what matters here.  for a while, i used the term social memory but i've learned it refers rather to obervational learning. so is there a term for it? what is the learning/memory type which requires active contribution called?  i observed i best memorize information when i actively compile it into meaningful hierarchy. that encompasses creating powerpoint presentations, explaining the topic to someone or writing summaries. i think my active contribution is what matters here.  for a while, i used the term social memory but i've learned it refers rather to obervational learning. so is there a term for it? the learning or memory type that requires active contribution from the learner is most commonly referred to as "active learning" or "active recall." in active learning, individuals engage directly with the material through activities such as summarizing, teaching others, making presentations, or creating concept maps. these strategies involve constructing meaningful relationships within the information, rather than passively consuming it. a closely related concept is "elaborative encoding," where the learner organizes new information into structured patterns or hierarchies, thereby creating stronger and more retrievable memory traces.

additionally, the techniques you describe—creating powerpoint presentations, explaining topics to others, or writing summaries— working memory is not completely distinct from short-term memory. it is a term that was used by miller et al. (1960) to refer to memory as it is used to plan and carry out behavior. one relies on working memory to retain the partial results while solving an arithmetic problem without paper, to combine the premises in a lengthy rhetorical argument, or to bake a cake without making the unfortunate mistake of adding the same ingredient twice.

is there a formal name for the compulsive and recurrent urges to pick one's nose in order to remove perceived imperfections?  under which formal category does the following condition fall?: the condition of experiencing frequent and compulsive urges to pick one's nose searching for any possible perceived imperfections (be they wet snot, dry snot and even scabs) in order to remove them or "smooth them out" by means of scratching, in a sort of endless pursuit for a sensation of perfect smoothness within one's nose (endless because the sensation of perfect smoothness is never quite achieved and usually the scratching itself leads to bleeding and scabs which paradoxically become the fuel for future nose picking sessions, in an endless cycle). references are very welcomed. is there a formal name for the compulsive and recurrent urges to pick one's nose in order to remove perceived imperfections?  under which formal category does the following condition fall?: the condition of experiencing frequent and compulsive urges to pick one's nose searching for any possible perceived imperfections (be they wet snot, dry snot and even scabs) in order to remove them or "smooth them out" by means of scratching, in a sort of endless pursuit for a sensation of perfect smoothness within one's nose (endless because the sensation of perfect smoothness is never quite achieved and usually the scratching itself leads to bleeding and scabs which paradoxically become the fuel for future nose picking sessions, in an endless cycle). references are very welcomed. certainly! here is a well-referenced passage answering your question:

---

the compulsive and recurrent urge to pick one's nose, specifically with the intention of removing perceived imperfections such as mucus, scabs, or to achieve a sensation of "smoothness," does have a formal clinical description. the behavior, commonly known as nose picking, is referred to in medical literature as **rhinotillexomania**. while nose picking is a prevalent habit among children and adults alike, **rhinotillexomania** refers specifically to the compulsive or pathological form, wherein the behavior becomes excessive, difficult to control, and causes significant episodes of skin picking are often preceded or accompanied by tension, anxiety, or stress. during these moments, there is commonly a compulsive urge to pick, squeeze, or scratch at a surface or region of the body, often at the location of a perceived skin defect.

my mind shows me my future [closed]  when i sleep, i can see some phenomenon as a dream. after some days or months or years that phenomenon happens in my life and forthwith my mind reminds me of that earlier dream that i saw. there are simple things such as for example getting down pen from my hand like wise.  my problem is how can this happen? i have heard this happens to others also. what kind of situation is this in the mind? is it normal or abnormal? my mind shows me my future [closed]  when i sleep, i can see some phenomenon as a dream. after some days or months or years that phenomenon happens in my life and forthwith my mind reminds me of that earlier dream that i saw. there are simple things such as for example getting down pen from my hand like wise.  my problem is how can this happen? i have heard this happens to others also. what kind of situation is this in the mind? is it normal or abnormal? many people report experiencing dreams that later seem to come true in real life, a phenomenon often referred to as déjà vu or precognitive dreaming. this experience is more common than you might think, and psychologists have several theories about why it happens. one possibility is that our brains are very good at recognizing patterns and making connections, so when something in real life resembles something we dreamed about, our minds link the two events—even if the dream itself wasn’t exactly the same. sometimes, we forget most of our dreams, but when a real-life event triggers the memory, it feels as though we “predicted” the future.

another explanation is that since then have had recurring dreams of being there and reliving the confusion i felt there and constant searching for why she treated me in the way she did as i was just left in the air. the dreams then trigger usually other negative dreams on other themes,death,rejection,loss, failure. i usualy end up feeling lost,unsure,distant after the dreams.

is there a term for the desire to maintain a level of anxiety?  as a war veteran who has/had ptsd, i've noticed in myself a desire to maintain a level of anxiety and stress closer to what i was used to.  you've probably also heard/seen this type of behavior from other veterans who engage in high risk behavior after separation from service.  i've also known former incarcerated individuals and phd graduates who do the same.  is there an official term for this behavior? further reading on the topic? is there a term for the desire to maintain a level of anxiety?  as a war veteran who has/had ptsd, i've noticed in myself a desire to maintain a level of anxiety and stress closer to what i was used to.  you've probably also heard/seen this type of behavior from other veterans who engage in high risk behavior after separation from service.  i've also known former incarcerated individuals and phd graduates who do the same.  is there an official term for this behavior? further reading on the topic? there is not a single "official" clinical term that precisely describes the desire to maintain a level of anxiety or stress reminiscent of a previous environment, such as combat or incarceration. however, this phenomenon has been recognized and discussed in psychological literature, especially in the context of trauma and post-traumatic stress disorder (ptsd).

one concept related to this is **"addiction to adrenaline"** or **"adrenaline seeking"**—a pattern where individuals, having grown accustomed to high-stress or high-alert environments, subconsciously or consciously seek out situations that recreate similar levels of arousal. this can manifest as engagement in risky behaviors ptsd is the re-experiencing and avoidance of trauma-related stressors, emotions, and memories from a past event or events that has cognitive dramatizing impact on an individual. ptsd can be attributed to the functional impairment of the prefrontal cortex (pfc) structure.

is it possible to over burden yourself?  if someone thinks a lot about their future like what are they going to do 1 year down the line or how will the live the rest of their lives and some of these ideas can be really extreme like taking a vow of celibacy in their thoughts. can they end up over-burdening themselves. also what are the symptoms of such a man/woman?  thanks in advance! is it possible to over burden yourself?  if someone thinks a lot about their future like what are they going to do 1 year down the line or how will the live the rest of their lives and some of these ideas can be really extreme like taking a vow of celibacy in their thoughts. can they end up over-burdening themselves. also what are the symptoms of such a man/woman?  thanks in advance! yes, it is entirely possible to overburden yourself, especially if you are constantly thinking about the future and putting pressure on yourself to meet extreme expectations or make rigid commitments. when someone frequently worries about what will happen a year from now or spends a lot of time planning every aspect of their life, it can lead to increased stress and anxiety. setting very demanding or unrealistic goals, such as making extreme vows or imposing strict rules on oneself, may actually lead to mental and emotional exhaustion.

symptoms of being overburdened in this way can include feeling constantly anxious or stressed, having trouble sleeping, experiencing mood swings, feeling overwhelmed by decisions, sometimes i feel lost just thinking about it! i think to travel like you do, you do have to have a strong sense of who you are and keep working towards the kind of person you want to be, like you said. sometimes, maybe we all feel we don’t know who we are….and i think travelling a lot can increase feelings of insecurity and ‘what am i doing?’

is there research to prove that humans achieve more when challenging ourselves or when we challenge others?  given that there is a single player game, let's say, tetris. i play the game for 5 days and my friend is also playing the game for 5 days. at the end of the 5 days we would like to see who has the highest score and that person wins some prize.  is it better that we see the scores of our opponent and try to better it, or is better that i do not see my friend's score and focus on just constantly beating my own score?  which of the 2 scenarios is likely to yield the higher score at the end of the 5 days? and why?  i'm doing some research on gamification and how competition can help us progress a skill. is there research to prove that humans achieve more when challenging ourselves or when we challenge others?  given that there is a single player game, let's say, tetris. i play the game for 5 days and my friend is also playing the game for 5 days. at the end of the 5 days we would like to see who has the highest score and that person wins some prize.  is it better that we see the scores of our opponent and try to better it, or is better that i do not see my friend's score and focus on just constantly beating my own score?  which of the 2 scenarios is likely to yield the higher score at the end of the 5 days? and why?  i'm doing some research on gamification and how competition can help us progress a skill. research in psychology and behavioral science suggests that both self-competition (trying to beat your own previous best) and social competition (competing against others) can motivate people to achieve more, but the effects and outcomes can differ depending on individual motivation and the context of the challenge.

**self-competition** (focusing on beating your own previous scores) draws on the concept of intrinsic motivation. studies have shown that when individuals are intrinsically motivated—meaning they are driven by personal growth, mastery, and self-improvement—they are more likely to persist in their efforts and experience deeper engagement and satisfaction (deci & ryan, 2000). masochism

is it a cognitive bias: "if you guess what i think you do, then what i think of you is true"  "if you've guessed what i am thinking (that you do / you have done / you will do / you are), then what i'm thinking of you is true" is this considered a cognitive-bias ? and if not what is it ?  without enough knowledge in psychology, i tend to think it is close to something like "dementia" / "paranoia" / "psychosis"  any pointers ? is it a cognitive bias: "if you guess what i think you do, then what i think of you is true"  "if you've guessed what i am thinking (that you do / you have done / you will do / you are), then what i'm thinking of you is true" is this considered a cognitive-bias ? and if not what is it ?  without enough knowledge in psychology, i tend to think it is close to something like "dementia" / "paranoia" / "psychosis"  any pointers ? passage:

the statement "if you guessed what i think you do, then what i think of you is true" does not directly correspond to an established cognitive bias, but it does reflect a type of distorted thinking. in psychology, a cognitive bias is a systematic pattern of deviation from norm or rationality in judgment, whereby inferences about other people and situations may be drawn in an illogical fashion. well-known examples include confirmation bias, where one favors information that confirms pre-existing beliefs, or the fundamental attribution error, where one overemphasizes personality-based explanations for others’ behavior.

the belief that your thoughts about another person become true is it a cognitive bias: 'if you guess what i think you do, then what i think of you is true' ... is this considered a cognitive-bias? and if not what is it? without enough knowledge in psychology, i tend to think it is close to something like 'dementia' / 'paranoia' / 'psychosis' any pointers?

opposite of white bear principle?  is there any phrase/term to describe the opposite of white bear principle (also known as ironic process principle)? i'm looking for a word to describe the process where something eludes you the more you try to think of it (or remember it). i think i may have come across a term to describe this phenomenon but i'm not sure if such a phrase/term exists. opposite of white bear principle?  is there any phrase/term to describe the opposite of white bear principle (also known as ironic process principle)? i'm looking for a word to describe the process where something eludes you the more you try to think of it (or remember it). i think i may have come across a term to describe this phenomenon but i'm not sure if such a phrase/term exists. certainly! here’s a passage answering your question:

the "white bear principle," also known as the ironic process theory, refers to the psychological phenomenon where trying to suppress certain thoughts makes them more persistent. the classic example is the instruction, "don’t think of a white bear," which causes most people to immediately think of a white bear. the opposite of this effect, where actively trying to remember or think of something causes it to become more elusive, is sometimes referred to as the "tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon" or "memory blocking." while there isn’t a universally accepted term that is precisely the direct opposite of the white bear opposite of white bear principle this seems very close, but according to the linked article, this is when repetition of the word causes disassociation of the word with its meaning. i think the question is about a related yet distinct phenomenon, where intense scrutiny (could be repetition, i suppose) causes disassociation of the written form of the word with the oral form (and possibly the meaning).

is there a special name for rejection of extremes in the list of cognitive biases?  is there a special name for the cognitive bias that causes a person choose a compromise solution even the extreme solutions are better or causes person prefer a middle value even the extreme values are more suitable? is there a special name for rejection of extremes in the list of cognitive biases?  is there a special name for the cognitive bias that causes a person choose a compromise solution even the extreme solutions are better or causes person prefer a middle value even the extreme values are more suitable? yes, there is a special name for the tendency to reject extreme options and favor a compromise or middle-ground solution, even when one of the extreme options might actually be better. this cognitive bias is known as the "compromise effect" or "extremeness aversion." the compromise effect describes the phenomenon where people are more likely to choose the middle option in a set of choices, perceiving it as more reasonable or safer compared to the extremes. this can lead to suboptimal decisions because the middle option is chosen not based on merit, but simply because it appears as a compromise. this bias often comes into play in consumer choices, negotiations regression extreme value,

how can someone enjoy something without dopamine?  andrew huberman says people and even animals with depleted dopamine levels can still enjoy stuff.  how does one enjoy without dopamine? isn't it the pleasure chemical?  can serotonin, oxytocin etc. contribute to pleasure experience like dopamine? how can someone enjoy something without dopamine?  andrew huberman says people and even animals with depleted dopamine levels can still enjoy stuff.  how does one enjoy without dopamine? isn't it the pleasure chemical?  can serotonin, oxytocin etc. contribute to pleasure experience like dopamine? while dopamine is often described as the “pleasure chemical,” its primary role is actually in motivation, reward anticipation, and reinforcement learning rather than directly generating pleasurable feelings. andrew huberman explains that even people or animals with depleted dopamine levels can still enjoy things, which suggests that pleasure is not solely dependent on dopamine. when dopamine levels are low, individuals may lack motivation to seek out rewarding experiences, but the capacity to feel pleasure when those experiences occur remains. this is because other neurochemicals, including serotonin, oxytocin, and endorphins, also contribute to the experience of enjoyment and pleasure.

serotonin is commonly associated with mood anhedonia is the inability to feel pleasure. it's a common symptom of depression as well as other mental health disorders. it's possible to still like eating chocolate ice cream or listening to jazz; you just don't like those things nearly as much as you used to for reasons you can't explain. scientists think anhedonia may be tied to changes in brain activity. you might have a problem with the way your brain produces or responds to dopamine, a 'feel-good' mood chemical.

functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fnirs) is a neuroimaging technique that measures changes in blood oxygenation in the brain by shining near-infrared light through the scalp and detecting how much light is absorbed as it passes from source to detector. to assess brain activity, fnirs focuses on tracking the concentration changes of oxygenated hemoglobin (hbo) and deoxygenated hemoglobin (hbr), the two main forms of hemoglobin that differ in how much near-infrared light they absorb.

the reason fnirs devices commonly use two different wavelengths (sometimes called "frequencies," but more precisely, why do fnirs devices commonly use two different frequencies? fnirs estimates the concentration of hemoglobin from changes in absorption of near infrared light.

the phenomenon you are describing is known as **"basking in reflected glory" (birging)**. birging refers to the tendency of individuals to enhance their self-image and feel a sense of pride or increased self-esteem when someone or something they are associated with, such as a sports team, celebrity, or political figure, achieves success or does something positive. people often express this by using inclusive language like "we won!" or by wearing team apparel after a victory. this psychological process allows individuals to feel connected to success even if they were not directly involved, and their self-confidence or social status may temporarily increase as a one of the most influential studies of this phenomenon was done by [robert cialdini] in 1976, known as the three (football) field study. he discovered that the students sought to have the success of their football team linked to them by wearing school-identified apparel. these students associated themselves with a success, even though they in no way affected or caused the success. through three different experiments, cialdini was able to demonstrate the birging phenomenon.

"group polarization" and "social segregation" are related concepts in the study of social dynamics, but they are not the same and refer to different phenomena. "group polarization" describes the process whereby individuals' opinions become more extreme after engaging in discussion with like-minded group members. as a result, the group's average opinion shifts toward a more extreme position than it held initially. in contrast, "social segregation" refers to the tendency for individuals with similar characteristics (such as opinions, backgrounds, or interests) to separate into distinct social groups, leading to a lack of interaction or mixing between different groups.

deflection of the basilar membrane  the basilar membrane becomes thicker and heavier from the basal end to the apical upper end - this is why high frequencies are perceived in the lower range and low frequencies in the upper range.  but why? why does the traveling wave of the membrane reach its maximum amplitude at high frequencies at the bottom and at low frequencies at the top? how is this related to the width, stiffness and mass of the basilar membrane? deflection of the basilar membrane  the basilar membrane becomes thicker and heavier from the basal end to the apical upper end - this is why high frequencies are perceived in the lower range and low frequencies in the upper range.  but why? why does the traveling wave of the membrane reach its maximum amplitude at high frequencies at the bottom and at low frequencies at the top? how is this related to the width, stiffness and mass of the basilar membrane? the deflection of the basilar membrane in the cochlea is a key mechanism for distinguishing different sound frequencies. the basilar membrane is structured so that it is narrower, stiffer, and lighter at the basal (bottom) end, and progressively becomes wider, more flexible, and heavier toward the apical (top) end.

when sound waves enter the cochlea, they create a traveling wave along the basilar membrane. high-frequency sounds cause the traveling wave to reach its peak amplitude near the basal end because the membrane here is stiff and resonant with these high frequencies. in contrast, low-frequency sounds travel further along the cochlea, note the gradual thickening of the basilar membrane from the base (20khz) to the apex (20hz). he suggested a role for the pillars of corti and a system of fibres stretched over the basilar membrane like piano strings. note that after having been superseded for a few decades by the 'travelling wave' theory, this first suggestion returns in another form.

in neuroscience and neural network literature, "recurrent" and "feedback" synapses refer to distinct types of connections, although they are sometimes conflated. recurrent synapses are connections within the same layer or between neurons of the same hierarchical level, forming lateral loops that allow information to be maintained and integrated over time. in contrast, feedback (or top-down) synapses are connections that transmit information from higher layers (or areas) back to lower layers in a hierarchical system. this means feedback connections play a role in carrying contextual or abstract information from later processing stages to influence earlier feedforward neural networks provide the dominant model... in this work, we investigate object recognition...

quasi-experimental designs, such as the pre-test post-test non-equivalent groups design, are essential in research precisely because randomization is not always possible or practical. while true experimental designs offer the advantage of random assignment, which helps control for confounding variables and increases internal validity, there are many real-world situations where randomization cannot be implemented. for example, in educational research, it may not be feasible or ethical to randomly assign students to different teaching methods or classrooms. similarly, in healthcare or organizational settings, random allocation to intervention the greatest advantages of quasi-experimental studies are that they are less expensive and require fewer resources compared with individual randomized controlled trials (rcts) or cluster randomized trials. quasi-experimental studies are appropriate when randomization is deemed unethical (e.g., effectiveness of hand hygiene studies).
